title: Name plaque in paper clay
subtitle: A clay plaque with carved or impressed lettering
excerpt: A paper clay name plaque is a flat slab with letters cut or impressed into the surface, pierced for hanging, and sealed once bone-dry. Paper clay holds crisp letter edges better than standard air-dry clay. Names, words, or short phrases work well. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slab plaque technique. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dry clay reinforced with cellulose fibres, which holds fine carved detail without cracking at letter edges.
The ideal drying stage for carving letters: firm enough to hold the carved wall cleanly, damp enough for the tool to cut smoothly.

content.text: is the right material for a name plaque because it holds the walls of carved letters without crumbling when it dries. Standard air-dry clay often cracks at the thin points of curved letters. The fibre in paper clay bridges those points and keeps them intact. The lettering is cut at the
stage rather than when wet, which gives a clean edge.

attrs.title: Planning the lettering
attrs.body: Sketch the letters in pencil on paper first and work out the spacing. Transfer the layout to the clay with the needle tool, making a light scratch for each letter position before cutting the final depth. Uppercase block letters are easier to carve than cursive. Allow 2 cm per letter as a minimum.

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails

content.text: Step 1: Roll and cut the slab

Roll the paper clay to 8 mm thick. Cut a rectangle sized to your text: allow at least 3 cm of border around the lettering on all sides. For a five-letter name in 2 cm letters, a plaque around 20 x 8 cm works well. Trim with the fettling knife against a ruler for straight edges.

Leave the slab uncovered on a canvas board for two to three hours until it reaches leather-hard. The clay should feel firm rather than tacky when touched and hold its shape when lifted by one corner without drooping.
Step 3: Sketch and cut the letters

Use the needle tool to scratch a faint baseline across the plaque at the correct height. Lightly sketch the letter outlines with the needle tool, these marks are a guide, not the final cut. With the sgraffito tool, cut each letter to a depth of 3 to 4 mm using firm, confident strokes. Clean the carved channel with the tip of the needle tool to remove loose clay.
Step 4: Pierce hanging holes and dry

Pierce two holes at the top of the plaque, 3 cm apart and 1.5 cm from the top edge. Dry between two boards for the first four hours, then in open air until bone-dry. Sand rough spots lightly. Seal with PVA diluted 1:1. Thread cord through the holes and tie.
Paint the whole plaque with a dark acrylic base, then sand the surface lightly so the raised clay shows pale and the recessed letters stay dark.
Use letter stamps pressed into the wet slab instead of carving, faster and more uniform for smaller text.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when making the name plaque.

items.cause: Clay was too dry before carving began.
items.fix: Mist the surface lightly with water and wait five minutes before continuing. The optimal carving window at leather-hard is approximately two to four hours after rolling, depending on room temperature.

items.cause: No baseline reference was scored before cutting.
items.fix: Score a faint baseline with the needle tool before any lettering. Use a second parallel line to mark the top of the letters. These guides are erased when the clay is sanded.
